bytes.topic: File Systems — VFS abstraction, ext4, btrfs, ZFS, journaling, copy-on-write
bytes.title: linux_kernel - Mastering VFS Abstraction for Optimized File System Performance
bytes.body: The Virtual File System (VFS) layer in linux_kernel abstracts file system operations, allowing developers to create and manage file systems without worrying about the underlying complexities. The VFS layer uses a modular design, where each file system is represented by a separate module. ext4 and btrfs are two popular file systems in linux_kernel, each with its own strengths and weaknesses. ZFS, a highly scalable file system, can be used with the ZFS on Linux (ZoL) kernel module. Journaling, a technique used by ext4 and btrfs, ensures data integrity by writing metadata and data in a single transaction. Copy-on-write (CoW) is another technique used by btrfs and ZFS, which creates a copy of the original data before modifying it.

bytes.topic: Kernel Architecture — monolithic vs microkernel vs hybrid, Linux vs BSD design

eBPF Use Cases — observability, networking (XDP), security (seccomp), tracing
bytes.title: linux_kernel eBPF Use Cases: Observability, Networking, Security, and Tracing
bytes.body: The Linux kernel's eBPF (extended Berkeley Packet Filter) subsystem offers a versatile tool for kernel developers. eBPF programs can be attached to the XDP (Express Data Path) hook to optimize network packet processing. For security, seccomp filters can be used to restrict process execution based on system calls. The kernel's tracing framework allows eBPF programs to collect and analyze kernel events. Observability can be improved by using eBPF programs to monitor system metrics, such as memory usage and CPU utilization.

bytes.title: linux_kernel: Taming FreeBSD with Jails and ZFS
bytes.body:
For developers familiar with linux_kernel's containerization using cgroups and namespaces, FreeBSD's jail() function offers a similar abstraction. Jails provide a secure, isolated environment for applications by assigning a unique IP address and port range. ZFS, a high-performance file system, integrates seamlessly with jails, allowing for efficient storage and snapshotting. The Ports collection, a comprehensive repository of pre-built packages, makes it easy to deploy and manage applications within jails. To create a jail on FreeBSD, use the following code:

jail -c -Z /path/to/jail -m "default"

This command creates a new jail with a default configuration, assigning it a unique IP address and port range.

bytes.title: linux_kernel - Monolithic vs Microkernel vs Hybrid: A Linux Perspective
bytes.body: Linux kernel follows a monolithic architecture, where the kernel space and user space are tightly coupled. This design allows for efficient memory management and fast context switching. However, it can lead to security vulnerabilities and scalability issues. In contrast, microkernels like L4 and Fiasco, separate kernel and user space, improving security but introducing significant overhead. Linux's hybrid approach, used in the Android kernel, combines the benefits of both. The Android kernel uses a combination of monolithic and microkernel designs, with the kernel space handling core functionality and user space handling device drivers. This allows for improved security and scalability.

bytes.topic: Linux Kernel — modules, kernel config, building, Kconfig/Makefile system
bytes.title: linux_kernel — Mastering Kconfig/Makefile for Module Development
bytes.body: The linux_kernel configuration system relies on Kconfig and Makefile to determine which modules to build. Kconfig files contain conditional statements that specify module dependencies. When a Kconfig file is updated, the Makefile is automatically regenerated. To build a specific module, navigate to its directory and run `make`. The Makefile will automatically include the necessary configuration files. To add a new module, create a Kconfig file and update the Makefile accordingly. Always run `make clean` before rebuilding to ensure a clean build process.

bytes.title: linux_kernel — Mastering Kernel Debugging with kgdb and ftrace
bytes.body: To debug kernel issues in linux_kernel, utilize kgdb for live kernel debugging. Configure kgdb by setting `kgdboc` in `/proc/sys/kernel/kgdboc`. Enable kernel tracing using ftrace, which provides detailed function call graphs. For performance analysis, employ perf_events. KASAN (Kernel Address Sanitizer) detects memory bugs. When a kernel crash occurs, examine the crash dump to determine the root cause.

bytes.title: linux_kernel Device Drivers 101: Character vs Block vs Network
bytes.body: Linux kernel device drivers are categorized into character, block, and network drivers. Character drivers interact with serial ports, terminals, and other character devices. They provide a file interface to access these devices. Block drivers, on the other hand, handle disk I/O operations. They provide a buffer cache and perform disk scheduling. Network drivers manage network interfaces and provide a socket interface to access network resources. To create a kernel module, you need to write a C file that includes the linux/module.h header and exports a module_init function.

bytes.title: linux_kernel WASM On Load: Wasmtime, Wasmer, and Plugin Systems
bytes.body: linux_kernel's Wasmtime and Wasmer projects enable server-side WebAssembly (WASM) execution. Wasmtime is a Rust-based runtime, while Wasmer is a C-based one. To integrate WASM on load into a linux_kernel module, use the Wasmtime or Wasmer APIs. For example, with Wasmtime, load a WASM module from a file and execute it with the `wasmtime_instantiate` function.

bytes.title: linux_kernel Containers: Namespaces and cgroups
bytes.body: linux_kernel uses namespaces to isolate process views and cgroups to control resource allocation. Namespaces (ns) are file system objects that group resources, such as PID, network, and mount points. They are used to create a logical separation between processes. cgroups (control groups) are used to limit resource usage by processes. A process is associated with a cgroup, which defines the resources it can access. linux_kernel provides two types of cgroups: hierarchy and flat. Hierarchy cgroups are used for creating nested resource hierarchies, while flat cgroups are used for simple resource allocation. linux_kernel also provides several cgroup controllers for managing resources such as CPU, memory, and network I/O.
